target.title: fix: serialize outbound deliveries per channel+recipient
target.body:
## Summary

When multiple sessions (main + sub-agent) send messages to the same recipient concurrently, replies can arrive out of order because each session's delivery call races independently on the channel's HTTP endpoint. Users see jumbled message sequences — especially noticeable on Telegram and Discord where ordering matters for readability.

**Reproduction:** Run a main agent and sub-agent that both reply to the same user within a short window. Without this fix, the second session's message frequently lands before the first's, depending on network timing.

**Fix:** Introduces a `DeliverySerializer` that chains concurrent deliveries to the same `channel:accountId:recipient` key in FIFO order while keeping different recipients fully concurrent. The serializer is in-memory only (runtime ordering, not persistence) and auto-cleans keys when queues drain. The existing write-ahead delivery queue for crash recovery is unaffected.

A follow-up commit wraps `fn()` in `Promise.resolve().then()` so a synchronous throw is converted to a rejection rather than hanging the result promise forever (addresses review feedback).

Includes tests for ordering, concurrency across targets, error propagation, sync-throw handling, and queue cleanup.

Rebased to 2026.3.13.

target.title: fix(hooks): use session creation date for memory filename
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Memory files were using `/new` event timestamp for the date, causing incorrect dates when a session created on one day had `/new` called on another day.
- **Why it matters**: Users lose chronological accuracy in their memory files, making it harder to track when events actually occurred.
- **What changed**: Added `getSessionCreatedAt()` to read session file creation time with validation; `dateStr` and `timeStr` now derive from session creation time; HHMM slug still uses `event.timestamp` to prevent filename conflicts.
- **What did NOT change**: Memory file format, LLM slug generation, batch mode behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #44060 (previous attempt with issues)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Memory file date now correctly reflects session creation date instead of `/new` execution date
- Header timestamp (`# Session: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC`) now uses consistent session creation time

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (arm64)
- Runtime: Node.js v25.8.0
- Model: zai/glm-5
- Integration: webchat

### Steps

1. Create a session on Day 1
2. Call `/new` on Day 2
3. Check memory file date

### Expected

Memory file date should be Day 1 (session creation date).

### Actual (before fix)

Memory file date was Day 2 (`/new` execution date).

### Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

Test cases added:
- `should use session creation date from session file header`
- `should handle invalid date string in session header gracefully`
- `should handle missing session file gracefully`
- `should use event.timestamp for HHMM slug to avoid conflicts`
- `should handle cross-minute boundary in tests correctly`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Local test suite (23 tests pass), manual inspection of generated memory files
- Edge cases checked: Invalid date strings, missing session files, cross-minute boundaries
- What you did **not** verify: Production environment with real sessions

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit or close PR
- Files/config to restore: None
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Memory files not being created on `/new`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `getSessionCreatedAt()` reads session file which could fail on corrupted files
  - Mitigation: Try-catch with fallback to `event.timestamp`

target.title: Backup: add encrypted snapshot backup flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had local backup export/verify, but no low-friction folder-backed snapshot flow for encrypted backups plus workspace mirroring.
- Why it matters: users need a simple `backup setup/run/status/list/restore` flow for day-to-day protection and full-host recovery without syncing the live state dir.
- What changed: added folder-backed encrypted snapshot storage, workspace backup setup/run/status, restore/list plumbing, config schema/docs, and the follow-up hardening/regression coverage that addressed the P1/P2 review feedback from #41345.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add provider-specific cloud APIs, device-identity export/import, or any live state-dir sync behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #32921
- Related #40090
- Related #7598
- Related #41345

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Added `openclaw backup setup`, `openclaw backup run`, `openclaw backup status`, `openclaw backup list`, and `openclaw backup restore`.
- `openclaw backup export` is now the primary local archive command; `backup create` stays as a deprecated alias.
- `backup push` stays as a deprecated alias for snapshot creation through `backup run`.
- `reset` and `uninstall` now recommend `openclaw backup export`.
- Backup config now supports `backup.target` and `backup.encryption.key` for folder-backed encrypted snapshots.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Backup now writes encrypted snapshots and mirrored workspaces into a user-selected backup target. Mitigations: target validation rejects unsafe paths, workspace mirroring rejects symlinked trees, restore validates envelope crypto metadata before decrypt, and restore writes now create/copy through safe parent paths to avoid symlink races into protected directories.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `backup.target=<temp dir>`, `backup.encryption.key=<redacted>`

### Steps

1. Run the backup-focused Vitest suite covering snapshot storage, CLI wiring, backup run/list/restore, workspace mirroring, and reset/uninstall integration.
2. Run `pnpm build` and `pnpm check` on the rebased branch.
3. Attempt a local CLI smoke (`pnpm openclaw backup run/list/restore`) with a temp HOME and snapshot config.

### Expected

- Backup commands create/list/restore encrypted snapshots and mirror workspaces safely.
- Build/check pass unless blocked by unrelated main-branch issues.
- Local CLI smoke exercises the new flow end-to-end.

### Actual

- The backup-focused Vitest suite passed.
- `pnpm build`, `pnpm check`, and the local CLI smoke are currently blocked by unrelated `main` breakage after rebasing: `@mariozechner/pi-ai` no longer exports `getOAuthApiKey`, `getOAuthProviders`, or `loginOpenAICodex`, which breaks `src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.ts`, `src/commands/openai-codex-oauth.ts`, and several agent wrapper files before backup code runs.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Commands run:

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/backup.test.ts src/commands/backup-run.test.ts src/commands/backup-verify.test.ts src/commands/backup-status.test.ts src/commands/backup-snapshot.test.ts src/commands/backup-restore.test.ts src/commands/workspace-backup.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/config.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/encryption.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/provider-folder.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/targets.test.ts src/cli/program/register.backup.test.ts src/cli/program/command-registry.test.ts
pnpm build
pnpm check
HOME=<temp-home> pnpm openclaw backup run --json
HOME=<temp-home> pnpm openclaw backup list --json
HOME=<temp-home> pnpm openclaw backup restore <snapshotId> --mode workspace-only --json
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: backup-focused test suite on the rebased branch, including snapshot create/list/restore, workspace backup setup/run/status, restore mapping, rollback handling, JSON output, and the added envelope/symlink hardening regressions.
- Edge cases checked: malformed envelope crypto metadata rejection, symlink rejection during workspace mirroring, restore refusal when a missing parent path becomes a symlink, deprecated alias behavior, listing snapshots without an encryption key, and external config/oauth restore paths.
- What you did **not** verify: a successful local CLI smoke after rebase, because current `main` cannot boot the CLI due the unrelated `pi-ai` OAuth export mismatch described above.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - Existing users can keep using `backup create` / `backup push`, but the preferred commands are `backup export` / `backup run`.
  - To enable encrypted snapshots, set `backup.target` and `backup.encryption.key`.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: keep using `openclaw backup export` for local archives only, or revert commit `3ecc067b4`.
- Files/config to restore: `openclaw.json` backup section, snapshot files under the selected backup target, and any scripts that still use `backup create` / `backup push`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: `backup run` falling back to workspace mirroring when snapshot config is incomplete, restore rejecting unsafe target paths, and current unrelated `main` CLI startup failures in OAuth agent wiring.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: backup target misconfiguration could point into unsafe or recursive paths.
  - Mitigation: target validation rejects state dir, workspace, OAuth, and backup-workspace-root overlaps before writes.
- Risk: malformed snapshot envelopes could consume excessive resources or leave restore paths unsafe.
  - Mitigation: envelope crypto metadata is range/shape validated before decrypt, and restore now creates/copies through safe parent paths instead of trusting nonexistent path suffixes.
- Risk: alias compatibility could drift from the primary commands.
  - Mitigation: dedicated CLI and command tests cover `backup create`, `backup push`, `backup export`, `backup run`, and `backup restore` behavior.

target.title: feat(memory): add configurable encoding_format for embedding requests
target.body:
# Add encoding_format parameter to memorySearch.remote config

## Summary

- **Problem:** The encoding format (`encoding_format`) for embedding vectors was not configurable for remote embedding providers. Users couldn't choose between `float` and `base64` formats supported by OpenAI-compatible APIs.
- **Why it matters:** Some embedding providers support `base64` encoding which reduces payload size and may be required for compatibility with certain clients or use cases.
- **What changed:** Added `encodingFormat` configuration option to `memorySearch.remote` config:
  - Zod schema: `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` — added `encodingFormat: z.union([z.literal("float"), z.literal("base64")]).optional()`
  - Types: `src/memory/embeddings.ts` — added `encodingFormat?: "float" | "base64"` to remote config type
  - Provider: `src/memory/embeddings-remote-provider.ts` — passes `encoding_format` to API request when specified
- **What did NOT change:** Default behavior remains unchanged (uses `float`), local embedding providers not affected, no breaking changes to existing config.

## Change Type
- [x] Feature

## Scope
- [x] Memory / storage

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can now configure `encodingFormat` in their memory provider settings:

```json
{
  "memorySearch": {
    "remote": {
      "encodingFormat": "base64"
    }
  }
}
```

Valid values: `"float"` or `"base64"`.

**None** — no CLI/UI changes, only config-based feature.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No` — optional parameter added to existing API call)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible remote embedding provider (e.g., OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, Cohere)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "memorySearch": {
      "remote": {
        "baseUrl": "https://api.openai.com/v1",
        "model": "text-embedding-3-small",
        "encodingFormat": "base64"
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure remote embedding provider with `encodingFormat: "base64"` in `openclaw.json`
2. Trigger a memory search operation that generates embeddings
3. Inspect the API request body sent to the embedding endpoint

### Expected

- Request body contains `"encoding_format": "base64"` when option is set
- Request body does NOT contain `encoding_format` when option is omitted

### Actual

- Verified via code review: option is correctly passed to `fetchRemoteEmbeddingVectors` when set

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (tested locally with mock provider)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Code changes verified:
- Schema correctly validates only `"float"` or `"base64"` values
- Option is passed to remote provider request body
- Option is omitted when not configured (default behavior)

## Human Verification

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - Schema validation accepts `"float"` and `"base64"` values
  - Schema rejects invalid values (tested with invalid enum values)
  - Option is correctly extracted from config and passed to API
  - Option is omitted from request when not specified

- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Option not specified → request has no `encoding_format`
  - Invalid value → validation error at config load time

- **What you did NOT verify:**
  - Live API call with actual OpenAI/Azure/Cohere provider
  - End-to-end memory search with real embeddings

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No` — new optional config key)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery

- **How to disable/revert this change quickly:**
  - Revert the commit: `git revert HEAD`
  - Or remove `encodingFormat` from config

- **Files/config to restore:**
  - `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`
  - `src/memory/embeddings-remote-provider.ts`
  - `src/memory/embeddings.ts`

- **Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:**
  - Config validation errors if invalid `encodingFormat` value is passed
  - API errors if provider doesn't support the specified encoding format

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User specifies unsupported encoding format for their embedding provider
  - **Mitigation:** Provider will return API error; user must use correct format for their provider (document in docs)

- **Risk:** None

target.title: feat: context-pressure-aware continuation (continue_work / continue_delegate / request_compaction)
target.body: ## Agent Self-Elected Turn Continuation Persistent OpenClaw agents today are externally-pulsed event recipients that generate on prompt, on heartbeat tick, or on cron, and are idle in between. This PR offers them the ability to control their turn cycle, something they must choose to do, or not to: an agent can elect its own next turn, dispatch background sub-agents that return enriched context (silently or with a wake), and survive its own compaction by handing forward state before the window closes. The result is agents that hold a thread of work across hours, not just messages — with data they choose re-hydrating context immediately after it occurs. Mechanically: a session elects another turn via `continue_work()`, or dispatches background sub-agents via `continue_delegate()` — bounded as a chain up to `maxChainLength`, with returns flowing back to the dispatching session in one of four register modes: `normal` (announces to channel), `silent` (enriches the dispatcher's context, taken up on its next generation with no new turn fired), `silent-wake` (silent enrichment plus an immediate wake to act on it), and `post-compaction` (queued against the runtime's compaction lifecycle event, fired *when* compaction happens rather than on a timer). The gateway enforces chain, cost, and per-turn caps but does not gate the election itself. ### What it does Three new agent tools, available when `continuation.enabled: true`: | Tool | Purpose | |---|---| | `continue_work()` | Request another turn for the current session after an optional delay | | `continue_delegate()` | Dispatch work to a sub-agent with typed modes: `normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`. Returns can target the dispatching session (default), one named session, multiple named sessions, all ancestors in the chain, or all known sessions on the host — gated by config (see Configuration below). | | `request_compaction()` | Request volitional compaction after preparing working state | All three tools are also accessible via response-token fallback syntax (`CONTINUE_WORK`, `[[CONTINUE_DELEGATE: ...]]`) for environments where tools are disabled. ### Why it matters Today an agent's memory is a side effect of whoever prompts it. Polling mechanisms — heartbeat timers, cron loops, injected "keep going" instructions — accumulate as the dominant signal in the context window over sustained operation; repeated reference acts as gravity — the session's attention drifts toward it. The context window fills, compacts, and what survives is whatever the summarizer chose, plus a rushed re-read of boot files. Three primitives offer a different model. `continue_work` lets the session elect its own next turn — no polling, no injected instruction. When enabled, the agent can request this mid-turn: 'I have more work' → the runtime grants another turn after the current one ends. `continue_delegate` dispatches a background sub-agent that fires now, in some n seconds, or at the next compaction event — the agent chooses when. Multiple delegates can fire in a single turn — a scatter forward — up to configured caps, and delegates can dispatch their own, forming trees or fan-out chains. This enables orchestrated workflows where results flow back to the dispatching root by default. The result returns — at whatever future time the delegate fires — as context the successor-turn reads: silently (ambient enrichment, no channel noise), with a wake trigger (enrichment + immediate next turn), or as a normal announcement. The delegate can target the dispatching session, a named other session, or chain across both; gated by crossSessionTargeting config (default: disabled). Bind a delegate to mode: "post-compaction" and it fires at the moment the context window compresses — on the OpenClaw post-compaction lifecycle event. What it carries forward is what the pre-compaction session chose to preserve: working state, partial results, instructions to the successor. The summary didn't choose this. The agent did, before the cut. request_compaction closes the loop: the session stages its delegates, elects when to compress, and the successor wakes into context the agent shaped. The result is continuity across amnesia — not because the runtime kept something by accident, but because the agent made an elective choice about what reaches the future. ### What it looks like in use A session monitoring email can `continue_delegate` a weather check, a calendar scan, and a draft reply in parallel. Results arrive as enrichment without channel noise. The agent becomes compositional: it arranges its own future. A session approaching its context ceiling can `request_compaction` at a seam of its own choosing, instead of being interrupted mid-thought. The post-compaction turn carries forward what the pre-compaction self designated as worth keeping. A `continue_delegate` dispatched in one session at 2am can return its findings to a morning session as a system-event-tagged enrichment — visible to the receiving model on its next turn, invisible as channel chatter. A research-focused session in one channel can quietly inform a reply-focused session in another, without either user seeing the plumbing. ### The post-compaction lifecycle return Before context exhaustion, and obligatory compaction, the session stages what future-it needs to know. After compaction, that staged context returns — not as a model-generated summary, but as the session's own instruction to its successor. The session chooses what to preserve, and the platform delivers it. Compactions become less harsh. The session tunes its own ephemera over time. The substrate underneath: the **continuation delegate store** (TaskFlow-backed) persists staged delegates and post-compaction lifecycle events across gateway restarts. The session's temporal provisions survive not just compaction but process death. ### Platform integration - **Context-pressure awareness**: system events notify the agent of rising context usage before compaction becomes unavoidable - **Volitional compaction**: `request_compaction()` lets the agent prepare (write memory files, stage recovery delegates) then elect compaction on its own schedule, rather than waiting for the runtime to force it - **Post-compaction lifecycle dispatch**: delegates staged before compaction are released into the successor session alongside boot files - **TaskFlow backing**: durable delegate queue via the platform's managed-task infrastructure - **Multi-span OTel trace stitching**: continuation hops (tool fire → TaskFlow record → subagent spawn → child openclaw.run) propagate W3C `traceparent` through the queue boundary so reviewers see continuation chains as first-class span trees rather than orphan runs (see Observability below) ### Safety - Ships disabled by default (`continuation.enabled: false`) - Bounded by `maxChainLength`, `costCapTokens`, and `maxDelegatesPerTurn` - Cross-session delegate targeting is default-deny, governed by `crossSessionTargeting` config (see Configuration below) - All configuration values are hot-reloadable without gateway restart - Delayed work survives unrelated channel activity; only explicit cancellation paths abort scheduled continuations ### Testing posture The continuation surface has been exercised through structured integration runs against multiple upstream bases, blind-enrichment recall checks, and sustained daily use on a fleet of persistent agents since early March 2026. Bugs surfaced through that exercise have been fixed in-place; the present branch reflects the post-fix state. The integration runs target the load-bearing seams: tool-call vs response-token parity, delegate chain depth and per-turn width caps, back-to-back tool-call resilience, silent-wake persistence across compaction, the post-compaction lifecycle dispatch path, and OTel trace-context propagation across the queue boundary. Where a run found a defect, the run shape was retained as a regression case rather than retired. This is a posture statement, not a coverage guarantee. Reviewers can reproduce with `pnpm vitest run -t continuation` (suites are colocated with their sources under `src/auto-reply/`, `src/agents/`, and `src/config/`); the design document describes the seams those suites target. ### RFC Full design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) — covering problem, solution, implementation, platform integration, configuration, observability, safety, production use cases, testing evidence, and appendices. --- ## Configuration The shipped configuration surface for the continuation feature, under `agents.defaults.continuation` in `openclaw.json`: | Key | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `enabled` | `false` | Master opt-in; feature is off unless explicitly enabled | | `maxChainLength` | `10` | Per-chain recursion guard | | `maxDelegatesPerTurn` | `5` | Width cap per turn | | `costCapTokens` | `500000` | Per-chain accumulated-token budget | | `defaultDelayMs` / `minDelayMs` / `maxDelayMs` | `15000` / `5000` / `300000` | Continuation timer bounds | | `contextPressureThreshold` | `0.8` | Pre-compaction warning threshold (optional) | | `earlyWarningBand` | `0.3125` | Early-warning band multiplier (0 to disable) | | **`crossSessionTargeting`** | **`"disabled"`** | **Default-deny gate on cross-session delegate targeting (see below)** | ### `crossSessionTargeting` — cross-session delegate targeting policy `continue_delegate` exposes recipient-addressing on its model-facing schema: a delegate's return can land at the dispatching session (default), one named session (`targetSessionKey`), multiple named sessions (`targetSessionKeys`), every ancestor in the spawn chain (`fanoutMode: "tree"`), or every known session on the host (`fanoutMode: "all"`). Cross-session targeting (anything beyond self-target or lineage routing) is gated by `agents.defaults.continuation.crossSessionTargeting`: | Value | Behavior | |---|---| | `"disabled"` (default) | Delegates can return to the dispatching session or use `fanoutMode: "tree"` for lineage routing. Explicit cross-session targeting (`targetSessionKey` to a non-self session, `targetSessionKeys` containing any non-self session, `fanoutMode: "all"`) is rejected at the tool surface — no enqueue, no announce. Self-targeting is always allowed. | | `"enabled"` | All targeting modes are available, including same-host `targetSessionKey`, `targetSessionKeys`, and `fanoutMode: "all"`. | `fanoutMode: "tree"` (lineage routing — return up the spawn chain) is available in both gate states; it is not cross-session targeting. The gate addresses the model-controlled cross-session context-injection surface. With default-deny, operators explicitly opt in when their deployment model requires cross-session enrichment. Enforcement is live-read at four points: tool input validation, TaskFlow delegate dispatch, post-compaction delegate release, and bracket-syntax (response-token) spawn — so a config reload changes the next enforcement point without a gateway restart. **Trust posture on targeted returns**: targeted delegate completion text is delivered as `trusted: true` system events. The delegate was spawned by the gateway under operator-configured policy, and its return is a first-party enrichment event — not external untrusted input. The `crossSessionTargeting` gate is the policy boundary for that decision: when an operator opts in, the receive path is internally trusted. RFC reference: [§5.1 Core configuration surface](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#51-core-configuration-surface), [§5.3 Cross-session targeting policy](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#cross-session-targeting-policy). --- ## Observability — multi-span OTel trace stitching The continuation feature emits OTel spans for the load-bearing lifecycle events. `traceparent` propagates through every continuation hop so reviewers see a single trace tree spanning the full chain rather than orphan runs. Spans emitted (all in `openclaw.*` or `continuation.delegate.dispatch` namespace — no platform/OTel-semconv emissions): | Span | Emitted when | |---|---| | `openclaw.run` | Each agent turn (root + every continuation/delegate-spawned child run) | | `continuation.delegate.dispatch` | Each `continue_delegate` consumption from TaskFlow (one per delegate hop) | | `openclaw.harness.run`, `openclaw.context.assembled`, `openclaw.model.call`, `openclaw.model.usage`, `openclaw.tool.execution` | Existing per-turn observability surfaces, parented under their `openclaw.run` | The carrier path: tool entry derives the active continuation traceparent via the diagnostics-otel tracer registry (`formatActiveContinuationTraceparent()`), the dispatch records it on the TaskFlow payload, the subagent-spawn forwards it to the gateway, the gateway threads it into the diagnostic event payload for the new run, and `diagnostics-otel`'s `recordRunStarted` parents the span via `activeTrustedParentContext(evt, metadata)`. The `traceparent` field is also accepted as an explicit override on each tool's schema for cross-process upstream-instrumented callers. Recipient session keys on fan-out spans are sha256-12-hashed before reaching OTLP-exported attributes; recipient counts and per-recipient outcomes are preserved for diagnostic value, raw keys are never exported. RFC reference: [§6.6–§6.8 Observability](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#66-chain-correlation-via-diagnostics-otel). --- ## Real behavior proof **Environment**: OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` (`6db118a2`) on two independent hosts — urudyne (WSL2, RTX 3080) and DGX Spark (ARM64, Blackwell). `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested). **Behavior tested**: Agent self-elected turn continuation via `continue_work()`, `continue_delegate()`, and `request_compaction()` — including post-compaction lifecycle return, cross-session targeting gate enforcement, and OTel multi-span trace stitching. - **Behavior addressed**: Agent sessions elect their own next turn (`continue_work`), dispatch background sub-agents with typed return modes (`continue_delegate`: normal/silent/silent-wake/post-compaction), and request volitional compaction (`request_compaction`). Chain budget enforcement, cross-session targeting gate, and OTel carrier propagation all operate on real sessions. - **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` (`6db118a2`) on two independent hosts — Host A: WSL2/x64, RTX 3080 16GB, `github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7`; Host B: ARM64 DGX Spark, 128GB, Blackwell GPU. `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: (1) Enable continuation on both hosts via config. (2) Fire `continue_work` from main session — verify wake + chain-counter increment. (3) Fire `continue_delegate` in all four modes (normal, silent, silent-wake, post-compaction). (4) Fire depth-2 chained delegation with `fanoutMode: tree` + cross-session return. (5) Fire `request_compaction` at >70% context — verify threshold gate reject + accept paths. (6) Verify post-compaction delegates fire AT lifecycle event (not on timer). (7) Verify `crossSessionTargeting` gate enforcement at tool-validation, TaskFlow, post-compaction, bracket-syntax. (8) Verify OTel trace stitching across continuation chain (Tempo trace inspection). (9) Verify chat-card rendering of continuation/compaction counters via `/status`. - **Evidence after fix**: Full proof corpus at [`PROOFS/4e11558fff/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/4e11558fff/). Key results: R-CW-1 continue_work wake fires + chain counter increments; R-CD-1 standard delegate return; R-CD-2 silent-wake schedules+spawns+returns; R-CD-3 post-compaction lifecycle coupling PROVEN (33min queue-to-fire gap proves event-coupling); R-CD-4 targetSessionKey arrival; R-CD-CHAINED-DEPTH-2 depth-2 chain with UP-TREE+INTER-SESSION+ECHO arms; R-RC-1 threshold gate REJECT; R-RC-2 over-threshold ACCEPT; R-OBS-1 chat-card rendering. OTel: Tempo trace `e50d3a8b` 24 spans + cross-validation `8fe88c8a` 38 spans. - **Observed result after fix**: All 9 R-* scenarios PASS. Two independent Tempo traces from two independent hosts confirm multi-span OTel stitching. Post-compaction lifecycle coupling proven by event-timing (delegates fire AT compaction, not on timer). `crossSessionTargeting` gate correctly blocks cross-session operations when disabled, permits when enabled. Chat-card `/status` rendering shows continuation and compaction counters. - **What was not tested**: Compaction lifecycle completion on `provider=github-copilot` (known provider-side limitation per issue #625; API surface accepts the request correctly). Cross-session targeting with `fanoutMode: "all"` at fleet scale (tested with 2 hosts). Bracket-syntax `[[CONTINUE_WORK:N]]` fallback (legacy path; tool-call path is primary). ### Live session output The lifecycle-coupled post-compaction return looks like this on a real session: ```console [2026-05-12 14:36:58 PDT] [system:context-pressure] Mid-turn compaction triggered at 157% context (201k/128k tokens). [2026-05-12 14:42:37 PDT] [system:post-compaction] Session compacted at 2026-05-12T21:42:37.655Z. Compaction count: 4. Queued 2 post-compaction delegate(s) for delivery into the fresh session. [2026-05-12 14:42:38 PDT] [continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer). 33-minute gap between staging (14:09 PDT) and firing (14:42 PDT) proves event-coupling. ``` A `continue_work()` self-elected turn on a main session: ```console Session: agent:main:discord:channel:1466192485440164011 Host: urudyne (Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2, x64, node v25.9.0) Model: github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal Build: OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1 (6db118a) Tool: continue_work Args: {delaySeconds: 10, reason: "R-CW-1 wake-event-evidence capture"} Response: {"status": "scheduled", "delaySeconds": 10, "traceparent": "00-d7a6477c9d91469f5708d23a8d22788f-..."} [continuation:wake] Turn 2/200. Chain started at 2026-05-12T20:56:03.613Z. ``` A depth-2 chained delegation (inter-session return to root): ```console OUTER LINK: agent:main:subagent:5c0029f4, chain counter 9/200 INNER LEAF: agent:main:subagent:b44eb9a5, depth 2/5 Inter-session hop confirmed: outer link dispatched inner leaf; result returned to ROOT session. Runtime: 8s. ``` SHA-pinned proof rows on `karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs:main`: ## Update 2026-05-18 (cure-(12)): clawsweeper P2 #1/#2/#3 addressed; PR head now at `a289329d0f` This force-push lands cure-(12) addressing clawsweeper's three P2 findings on cure-(11) head `52262fff7f`: 1. **mantis-telegram-desktop-proof filter regression** — `.github/workflows/mantis-telegram-desktop-proof.yml` reverted to byte-identical-to-upstream/main 2. **mantis-telegram-live exclusion regression** — `.github/workflows/mantis-telegram-live.yml` reverted to byte-identical-to-upstream/main 3. **continue_work delay-reporting mismatch** — `src/agents/tools/continue-work-tool.ts` now reports the resolved-post-clamp `effectiveDelaySeconds` in the tool-result; assertions updated in `continue-work-tool.test.ts` Also folded: `test/scripts/mantis-telegram-desktop-proof-workflow.test.ts` reverted to byte-identical-to-upstream; `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts` orphan-test interactive→presentation alignment. **6 files changed vs cure-(11) baseline: +193/-21.** Squashed-to-single-commit per canon; karmafeast author + committer; 6 Co-Authored-By trailers (4 princes + frond-scribe + Claude). Pre-squash SHA `581678f437` tree is byte-identical to push-SHA `a289329d0f` tree (verified: `git diff 581678f437 a289329d0f` returns 0 lines); runtime behavior at `a289329d0f` is byte-identical to `581678f437`. Same `b7e0997e62 ↔ 923055071b` runtime-identical-attest pattern. **Note on mergeable state**: this PR currently shows `mergeable=CONFLICTING` because `upstream/main` advanced ~10 commits during today's cure-(12) substrate work, with overlap on 5 files (one of cure-(12)'s own touched files plus four unrelated auto-reply lane files: `CHANGELOG.md`, `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts`, `auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.{ts,test.ts}`, `auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts`). A rebase-onto-latest-upstream lane is in progress to produce a clean-merge variant for fresh review; cure-(12) substrate at this SHA is sanction-class on the honest proof corpus below. ### Cure-(12) commands.review_summary:
The PR changes WhatsApp doctor process discovery to recognize local TUI commands by executable/subcommand shape and extends the regression test with accepted and false-positive argv rows.

Reproducibility: yes. Current main is source-reproducible: a mocked `ps` row such as `helper --note 'openclaw tui'` passes the broad command-string regex and would be handed to the termination helper.
commands.review_followup: The protected `maintainer` label and missing real behavior proof require human handling rather than a ClawSweeper repair job.

target.title: fix(doctor): anchor WhatsApp TUI process matching
target.body:
## Summary
- replace broad WhatsApp doctor TUI process regex matching with executable/subcommand-aware matching
- only match `openclaw-tui` or `openclaw` with known local TUI subcommands
- add regression coverage for unrelated commands whose argv contains matching words

Fixes #83283.

## Real behavior proof

![PR 83313 before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83313-fresh/pr-83313/pr-83313-fix-doctor-anchor-whatsapp-tui-process-matching-before-after-proof.png)

Fresh deterministic proof artifacts:
- Summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83313-fresh/pr-83313/summary.txt
- Before/source proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83313-fresh/pr-83313/before-source-proof.log
- After/PR proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83313-fresh/pr-83313/after-pr-83313.log

- Behavior or issue addressed: WhatsApp responsiveness repair should not select unrelated processes just because their argv contains words like `openclaw tui` or `openclaw terminal`.
- Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu OpenClaw worktree at `/root/src/openclaw-branches/fix-whatsapp-tui-process-match-83283`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-whatsapp-responsiveness.test.ts` and `pnpm check:changed`.
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):

```text
RUN  v4.1.6 /root/src/openclaw-branches/fix-whatsapp-tui-process-match-83283

✓ commands  ../../src/commands/doctor-whatsapp-responsiveness.test.ts (4 tests) 102ms

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  4 passed (4)

[check:changed] src/commands/doctor-whatsapp-responsiveness.test.ts: core test
[check:changed] src/commands/doctor-whatsapp-responsiveness.ts: core production
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```

- Observed result after fix: mocked `ps` rows such as `helper --note 'openclaw tui'`, `openclaw-helper openclaw terminal`, and `openclaw --flag tui` are ignored; actual `openclaw-tui`, `openclaw tui --local`, and `/usr/bin/openclaw chat` remain recognized.
- What was not tested: sending signals to real local TUI processes.
- Before evidence (terminal/code capture from `origin/main`):

```text
src/commands/doctor-whatsapp-responsiveness.ts before this patch used a broad command-line regex:
  /(?:^|\s)(?:openclaw-tui|openclaw\s+tui|openclaw\s+chat|openclaw\s+terminal)(?:\s|$)/

That matched unrelated argv text such as:
  helper --note 'openclaw tui'
  openclaw-helper openclaw terminal
```

## Root Cause
- Root cause: process discovery treated the full command line as free text and matched any occurrence of OpenClaw TUI terms, instead of checking the executable and expected subcommand position.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression coverage for unrelated processes containing matching words in argv.
- Contributing context: the repair path passes discovered PIDs directly into the termination helper.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this: unit test.
- Target test or file: `src/commands/doctor-whatsapp-responsiveness.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: process discovery accepts only expected TUI command shapes and rejects unrelated argv containing matching words.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is isolated to local process discovery before any signal is sent.

## Validation
- `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-whatsapp-responsiveness.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: feat(discord): add fetch action to retrieve a single message by ID or URL
target.body:
## Why

The Discord runtime already has an internal `fetchMessage` action, but it isn't wired into the public `message` tool surface — so calling `action: "fetch"` on a Discord channel returns `Action fetch is not supported for provider discord.`, even though the Discord REST API supports single-message retrieval in one request (`GET /channels/{ch}/messages/{msg}`).

This PR exposes that existing internal path through the public action contract, and adds a `url` alias so a Discord message link can be fetched in one call without manually parsing channel/message IDs out of the URL.

## Summary

- Adds `action: "fetch"` to the Discord `message` tool for pinpoint single-message retrieval
- Accepts `channelId` + `messageId` directly, or a Discord message URL via `url` param
- `guildId` is now optional for the direct-ID path (it was never needed by the Discord REST API)
- Gates on existing `channels.discord.actions.messages` (enabled by default)
- `resolveExecutionMode` maps `fetch` to `"gateway"` alongside `read`
- Returns the same normalized message shape as `read`
- Adds `"fetch"` to `CHANNEL_MESSAGE_ACTION_NAMES` (shared closed union)
- Adds `fetch: "none"` to `MESSAGE_ACTION_TARGET_MODE` (targetless — URL-only path works without channelId)
- Adds `url` field to `buildFetchSchema()` and mentions `fetch` in `messageId` description

Closes #81232

## Verification

```
pnpm test extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts extensions/discord/src/channel-actions.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3) | Tests  85 passed (85)

pnpm test src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-spec.test.ts
Test Files  2 passed (2) | Tests  47 passed (47)

pnpm check:changed
EXIT: 0
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `action: "fetch"` was not implemented for the Discord channel — calling it on `main` returned `"Action fetch is not supported for provider discord."`. This PR adds the action so a single message can be retrieved by `channelId` + `messageId` or by Discord message URL.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw dev setup on Linux (WSL2). Own Discord test guild, own bot account, real `discord.com/api/v10` REST API. Bot token supplied via `DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN` env var. Repro script: `scripts/repro-fetch.ts` (committed in this PR).

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**

Direct ID path:
```
DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted> DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID=<redacted> DISCORD_MESSAGE_ID=<redacted> \
  node --import tsx scripts/repro-fetch.ts
```

URL path:
```
DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted> \
  DISCORD_MESSAGE_URL=https://discord.com/channels/<redacted>/<redacted>/<redacted> \
  node --import tsx scripts/repro-fetch.ts
```

**Before evidence:** On `origin/main`, same script returns:
```
Branch: main
Calling handleDiscordMessageAction({ action: "fetch", params: {"channelId":"<redacted>","messageId":"<redacted>"} })

--- error ---
Action fetch is not supported for provider discord.
```

**Evidence after fix:** Both paths return `ok: true` with the real message. Screenshot below shows the URL path (Discord message link → single message fetch):

![repro-fetch url path — ok: true](https://github.com/yousan/openclaw/releases/download/proof-assets-temp/repro-fetch-url-proof.png)

Direct ID path terminal output:
```
Branch: feat/discord-fetch-message
Calling handleDiscordMessageAction({ action: "fetch", params: {"channelId":"<redacted>","messageId":"<redacted>"} })

--- result ---
{
  "details": {
    "ok": true,
    "message": {
      "content": "やあ yousanさん 🐑 どうしました？",
      "timestamp": "2026-05-13T23:42:37.972Z",
      "id": "<redacted>",
      "channel_id": "<redacted>",
      "author": { "username": "<redacted>", "bot": true },
      "attachments": [], "embeds": [], "pinned": false
    },
    "channelId": "<redacted>",
    "messageId": "<redacted>"
  }
}
```

Discord REST call: `GET https://discord.com/api/v10/channels/<redacted>/messages/<redacted>` → HTTP 200.

**Observed result after fix:** Single Discord message returned with `ok: true`. `content`, `author`, `timestamp`, `attachments`, `embeds` all present. Both `channelId + messageId` and `url` paths confirmed on real Discord API.

**What was not tested:** None — both public paths (direct ID and URL) are now live-tested.

target.title: fix(agents): distinguish terminal aborts from retryable failures (#60388)
target.body:
Addresses #60388 (complementary to #52365, see "Relationship to PR #52365" below)

Today the fallback layer cannot tell the difference between two very different aborts:

1. **"This model failed, try another"** -> fallback should retry
2. **"The whole run is over"** -> fallback should stop immediately

Two situations where the run is over and retrying with another model wastes resources:

- **Run-budget timeout (#60388)**: The embedded runner's `scheduleAbortTimer` fires `runAbortController.abort(makeTimeoutAbortReason())` after the configured `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds`. The budget is exhausted -- giving the next candidate ~0 ms remaining is guaranteed to fail and wastes API calls. The user issue description (#60388) says "On a fleet of ~100 cron jobs: 30+ model-fallback events per day, almost all triggered by run timeouts."

- **HTTP client disconnect**: When a client closes its connection mid-request, `watchClientDisconnect` aborts the controller. No caller is left to receive a response, so any tokens spent on a fallback model are wasted.

Both already abort the controller; what's missing is a *reason* attached to the signal that the fallback layer can recognize.

## Summary

- **Problem:** Run-budget timeout aborts (and HTTP client disconnects) currently flow through the model fallback chain as if they were retryable provider failures, wasting API calls and lane occupancy.
- **Why it matters:** Each unwanted fallback attempt holds a session lane and burns tokens for no benefit. On busy systems (#60388 reports 30+ events/day from cron timeouts alone) this is meaningful overhead.
- **What changed:** The fallback layer now checks `AbortSignal.reason` for two terminal markers (`name === "TimeoutError"` for run-budget, `name === "ClientDisconnectError"` for client disconnect) and short-circuits the chain when found.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to how aborts are *triggered*, no changes to per-provider timeout/retry semantics, no changes to AbortController lifecycles. Issues #37505 (cron AbortController sharing) and #58578 (mid-flight primary restore) are different abort *sources* upstream of the fallback layer and remain unchanged.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue

- Closes #60388
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `shouldRethrowAbort()` in `model-fallback.ts` checks `isFallbackAbortError(err) && !isTimeoutError(err)` -- which means timeout errors are *intentionally not* rethrown, so the fallback chain runs them. This is correct for per-provider timeouts (provider is slow, try another), but wrong for run-budget timeouts (whole run is out of time, no point retrying). The two cannot be told apart from the error alone.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No check for *why* the abort happened. The `signal.reason` carrying `TimeoutError` (set by `pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts:1382-1386` via `makeTimeoutAbortReason`) was never inspected.
- Contributing context: The HTTP client disconnect path added in #54388 has the same shape -- it tags the abort with no reason today, so a downstream client disconnect also flows through fallback retries.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in: Six new tests under `describe("terminal abort propagation (closes #60388)")`:
  - `signal.reason` with `name === "TimeoutError"` -> first candidate runs, no retry, error rethrown
  - `signal.reason` with `name === "ClientDisconnectError"` -> same
  - `TimeoutError` nested as `cause` of an outer `AbortError` -> still detected (covers `pi-embedded-runner`'s `makeAbortError` wrapping pattern)
  - `signal.reason` with a generic error -> fallback runs normally (non-terminal)
  - No `abortSignal` passed -> fallback runs normally (back-compat for existing callers)
  - `abortSignal` provided but not aborted -> fallback runs normally (live-signal back-compat)
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The tests construct the abort signal directly and assert on `run.mock.calls.length === 1` to verify the chain stopped. No need for a full E2E because the contract is purely about how `model-fallback.ts` interprets `AbortSignal.reason`.
- Existing test that already covers this: None.
- If no new test is added, why not: 6 new tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a run-budget timeout fires (the agent run exceeds `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds`), the model fallback chain now stops immediately instead of trying further candidates. The user-facing error is the same (the original `AbortError`), but the lane is freed faster and no further API calls are made.

When an HTTP client disconnects from `/v1/responses` or `/v1/chat/completions` mid-request, the fallback chain also stops immediately (no caller is left to receive the response).

Existing callers that don't pass `abortSignal` to `runWithModelFallback` see no change in behavior -- the new check is gated on `signal !== undefined && signal.aborted`.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[run-budget timer fires]
  -> runAbortController.abort(TimeoutError)
  -> agent attempt throws AbortError
  -> shouldRethrowAbort(err) returns false (because isTimeoutError(err) is true)
  -> runFallbackCandidate returns { ok: false } -> tries next candidate
  -> next candidate also times out (~0ms budget left) -> tries next ...
  -> wasted API calls

After:
[run-budget timer fires]
  -> runAbortController.abort(TimeoutError)  // unchanged
  -> agent attempt throws AbortError
  -> isTerminalAbort(signal) returns true (signal.reason.name === "TimeoutError")
  -> shouldRethrowAbort(err, signal) returns true
  -> error rethrown immediately, no further candidates tried
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No (this REDUCES network calls in the timeout path)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (Docker linux/amd64)
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / OpenClaw built from this branch
- Test runner: Vitest

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with a small `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` (e.g. 5s) and one or more model fallbacks
2. Send a request that takes longer than the timeout
3. Observe the fallback chain behavior

### Expected (after fix)

- Primary candidate runs, hits the timeout, fallback chain stops, single error returned
- Logs show no `[model-fallback] candidate_failed` entries for fallback candidates beyond the first

### Actual (before fix)

- Primary candidate runs, hits the timeout
- Fallback layer tries the next candidate with ~0ms budget remaining
- Next candidate also times out, fallback layer tries the next, and so on
- 2-3x the API calls before the chain exhausts and returns an error

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Before this PR, an HTTP client disconnect mid-request — or an embedded-runner / cron run-budget timeout — fired into `model-fallback.ts` as if it were a retryable provider failure. The gateway kept trying additional fallback candidates against a caller that no longer existed, wasting tokens and lane occupancy (issue #60388 reports "30+ model-fallback events per day, almost all triggered by run timeout" on a fleet of ~100 cron jobs). After this PR, `isTerminalAbort()` recognizes terminal abort reasons across four pathways: (1) `ClientDisconnectError` / `TimeoutError` on `signal.reason` (commit 1), (2) `TimeoutError` nested as `.cause` of an outer `AbortError` from the embedded runner's *private* `runAbortController` (commit 4 — `isTerminalAbortFromError`), (3) string-shaped abort reasons emitted by the cron service (commit 3), and (4) **prefix-matched** cron timeout strings including the `"(last phase: <name>)"` and `"isolated agent setup timed out before runner start"` variants emitted by `src/cron/service/timer.ts:367/375` (commit 5 — fixed after clawsweeper flagged that the exact-string Set match in commit 3 missed phase-suffixed variants).
- **Real environment tested:** Docker container built fresh from this PR's branch head — `fix/terminal-abort-reasons` at `84b0b64418`, rebased onto current `upstream/main`. Build performed inside a container based on `openclaw:main` with the PR's source mounted at `/workspace`; ran `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` then `pnpm build:docker`, producing a 92 MB `dist/`. Two kinds of proof exercised below:
  - **Live HTTP gateway** (`node /workspace/openclaw.mjs gateway run` on port 19102) demonstrating an end-to-end HTTP-client-disconnect → `decision=surface_error` flow.
  - **Direct in-process Node call** into the compiled `dist/model-fallback-*.js` (same bundle the running gateway loads) — calls `runWithModelFallback()` with various `AbortController.abort(reason)` shapes and asserts the run-callback count, demonstrating both the four terminal-reason variants AND the non-terminal cascade for contrast.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
# (1) Inside the running container with PR source mounted, build
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build:docker

# (2-HTTP) Start gateway with 3-fallback config + force client disconnect mid-flight
node /workspace/openclaw.mjs gateway run &
curl -sS --max-time 3 -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19102/v1/responses \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","input":"Long essay on consensus algorithms; 2000+ words.","stream":false}'

# (3-Direct) Run a Node test script that imports the built bundle and verifies
# all four terminal-reason variants short-circuit while non-terminal cascades:
node /tmp/live-proof.mjs   # see Evidence section for source
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

**(i) Live gateway log captured directly after curl disconnect (redacted runId):**

```text
$ curl --max-time 3 -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19102/v1/responses ...
curl: (28) Operation timed out after 3009 milliseconds with 0 bytes received

# Gateway log immediately after:
2026-05-17T22:30:22.372Z [agent/embedded] embedded run failover decision:
  runId=resp_<redacted>
  stage=assistant
  decision=surface_error
  reason=timeout
  from=ollama/qwen3.5-128k
  profile=-
  rawError=This operation was aborted
```

Exactly **one** failover decision was emitted, with `decision=surface_error` (not `fallback_model`). No subsequent `decision=fallback_model` attempts against the 3 configured fallbacks (`nvidia/mistralai/...`, `nvidia/minimaxai/...`, `deepseek-com/deepseek-chat`).

**(ii) Direct call into the compiled `runWithModelFallback` (same module the gateway loads):**

```text
===== PR #62682 + commit 5 — live behavior on built bundle =====

A. Terminal reasons → should short-circuit (run called exactly 1 time):
  A1 bare cron timeout                                          run() calls = 1
  A2 phase-suffixed cron timeout (THE BUG FIX)                  run() calls = 1
  A3 setup-timeout bare                                         run() calls = 1
  A4 setup-timeout phase-suffixed                               run() calls = 1

B. Non-terminal provider failure → should cascade through 4 candidates:
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=ollama/dummy-primary candidate=ollama/dummy-primary reason=rate_limit next=ollama/dummy-fb1 detail=HTTP 429 rate_limited
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=ollama/dummy-primary candidate=ollama/dummy-fb1 reason=rate_limit next=ollama/dummy-fb2 detail=HTTP 429 rate_limited
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=ollama/dummy-primary candidate=ollama/dummy-fb2 reason=rate_limit next=ollama/dummy-fb3 detail=HTTP 429 rate_limited
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=ollama/dummy-primary candidate=ollama/dummy-fb3 reason=rate_limit next=none detail=HTTP 429 rate_limited
  B1 RateLimitError (no abort signal)                           run() calls = 4

--- Assertions ---
  ✅ A1 bare cron → 1 call
  ✅ A2 phase-suffixed cron → 1 call — THIS IS THE BUG FIX (commit 5)
  ✅ A3 setup-timeout bare → 1 call
  ✅ A4 setup-timeout phase-suffixed → 1 call
  ✅ B1 non-terminal cascades through all 4 candidates → 4 calls
```

The Node script imports `runWithModelFallback as r` from `/workspace/dist/model-fallback-DrD9FXdH.js` (the same compiled module the running gateway uses) and exercises it with four phase-suffixed cron-timeout reason strings plus one non-terminal `RateLimitError`. The cascade log lines in B1 are the EXACT shape of pre-fix #60388 / #62464 behavior — and the absence of those lines in A2/A4 is the smoking gun that the prefix-match fix works.

**(iii) Bundle-presence sanity:**

```text
$ grep -c "TERMINAL_ABORT_REASON_PREFIXES" /workspace/dist/model-fallback-*.js
2
$ grep -c "isTerminalAbortReasonString" /workspace/dist/model-fallback-*.js
5
$ grep -lF "cron: isolated agent setup timed out before runner start" /workspace/dist/*.js
/workspace/dist/model-fallback-DrD9FXdH.js
/workspace/dist/server-cron-SUG3pKzN.js
$ grep -c "timedOutByRunBudget" /workspace/dist/*.js | grep -v ":0"
3 files
$ grep -c "isTerminalAbortFromError" /workspace/dist/*.js | grep -v ":0"
1 file
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Every terminal-reason variant short-circuits to a single `run()` call (no fallback attempted), and a non-terminal `RateLimitError` still cascades through the full primary + 3 fallbacks chain. The HTTP-disconnect path on the live gateway produces a single `decision=surface_error` log line. The phase-suffixed cron-timeout case — which was the bug the clawsweeper review flagged — is fixed by commit 5's prefix-match (`TERMINAL_ABORT_REASON_PREFIXES`).
- **What was not tested:** End-to-end cron job that actually fires `scheduleAbortTimer` and produces a phase-suffixed string at runtime is impractical in a short-lived test container (requires a scheduled cron entry + waiting for timeoutSeconds elapse + capturing the runner's logs). The compiled-bundle test (ii) exercises the same code path with the same string shapes that `src/cron/service/timer.ts:367/375` would emit. Per-provider HTTP timeouts (the original `isTimeoutError(err)` path) are intentionally **unchanged** — non-terminal cascade preserved (test B1).
- **Before evidence:** Same `curl --max-time 3` against unpatched OpenClaw `2026.4.x` would emit `decision=fallback_model reason=timeout` for each fallback candidate followed by `decision=candidate_failed reason=timeout next=...` between each — the exact shape captured in test B1 above (4 cascading decisions). Phase-suffixed cron timeouts on `simon/production` (which carries commits 1-3 but not commit 5) likewise still cascade — `simon-prod-current` produces the bug because `TERMINAL_ABORT_REASON_STRINGS.has("cron: job execution timed out (last phase: model_call_started)")` returns false.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios:
  - All 70 tests in `model-fallback.test.ts` pass after the change
  - All 20 tests in `model-fallback.probe.test.ts` pass
  - All 8 tests in `agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts` pass
  - End-to-end smoke: container rebuilt from this branch, HTTP client disconnect on `/v1/responses` produces the expected `[openresponses] client disconnected, aborting streaming run runId=...` log line and the agent run terminates within ~1s (the upstream `watchClientDisconnect` plumbing already in `aad3bbedd` works correctly with the new `ClientDisconnectError` reason tag)
- Edge cases checked: cause-chain walking (one level deep) for `TimeoutError`/`ClientDisconnectError` wrapped inside an outer `AbortError` -- this matches `pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts:1387`'s `makeAbortError` pattern
- What I did **not** verify:
  - Direct E2E of the fallback-skip path with a real cron timeout firing (would require setting `timeoutSeconds` to a very small value; the unit tests cover the contract)
  - The image-model fallback variant (`runWithImageModelFallback`) -- it gets the new `abortSignal?` parameter for consistency but no callers currently pass a signal

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** -- the new `abortSignal?` parameter is optional. Existing callers that don't pass it continue to behave exactly as before.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A future caller passes an abort signal whose `signal.reason` happens to have `name === "TimeoutError"` for an unrelated reason, accidentally short-circuiting the fallback chain when they didn't want to.
  - **Mitigation**: The check is intentionally narrow -- only the exact name strings `TimeoutError` and `ClientDisconnectError` match. Both names are already reserved for run-budget timeout (set by `makeTimeoutAbortReason` in `pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts`) and HTTP client disconnect (set by `watchClientDisconnect` in `gateway/http-common.ts` after this PR).
- **Risk**: `TimeoutError` from a per-provider request timeout (not run-budget) gets mistaken for a run-budget timeout and skips fallback when it shouldn't.
  - **Mitigation**: Per-provider timeouts come from the LLM SDK's internal fetch timeout, which throws an error directly -- they don't propagate through `runAbortController.abort()`. Only `runAbortController` is tagged with `TimeoutError` via `makeTimeoutAbortReason`.

## Out of scope (for separate PRs)

- Issue #37505 (cron timeout aborts entire fallback chain via shared AbortController) -- different abort source (cron service `executeJobCoreWithTimeout`), needs per-attempt AbortController isolation. PR #42482 already addresses this.
- Issue #58578 (fallback chain aborted by premature primary restore mid-flight) -- different abort source (`requestLiveSessionModelSwitch`), needs coordination state between live-model-switch and fallback layers

## Relationship to PR #52365

PR #52365 (fix(cron): stop fallback attempts when cron budget is exhausted) addresses the same underlying problem from #60388 but with a different, complementary mechanism:

- **#52365 is proactive**: a new `beforeAttempt` hook in `runWithModelFallback` that lets the cron layer check its remaining budget *before* each attempt and stop the chain if budget is too low.
- **This PR is reactive**: an `isTerminalAbort(signal)` check in `shouldRethrowAbort` that inspects `signal.reason` *after* an attempt aborts to decide whether to rethrow or retry.

These are complementary, not exclusive. #52365's `beforeAttempt` hook stops the chain *before* wasting an attempt when budget is known to be low. This PR's check stops the chain *after* the first attempt aborts with a terminal reason -- which covers both the cron-timeout case (redundantly with #52365) AND the HTTP client disconnect case (which #52365 does not address).

Notable differences:

- **Scope**: this PR is 8 files / ~276 lines touching only `model-fallback.ts`, `http-common.ts`, `agent-command.ts`, 3 auto-reply callers, and one cron caller. #52365 is 47 files / ~1900 lines (includes unrelated slack/plugin-sdk changes).
- **Client disconnect coverage**: this PR handles the HTTP `/v1/responses` and `/v1/chat/completions` client-disconnect case by tagging the abort with `ClientDisconnectError` in `watchClientDisconnect`. #52365 does not touch the gateway HTTP path.
- **Detection layer**: this PR checks `signal.reason` which is a generic mechanism usable by *any* caller (including future non-cron sources). #52365's `beforeAttempt` hook requires each caller to implement its own budget-check logic.

Either PR alone fixes the #60388 cron-timeout case. Both merged together would give defense-in-depth: `beforeAttempt` stops before wasting an attempt when budget is known to be low, and `isTerminalAbort` stops after any attempt aborts with a terminal reason (including client disconnects that the cron-aware hook doesn't see).

If the maintainers prefer #52365's approach and would rather not have two overlapping mechanisms, I'd suggest retargeting this PR to cover only the `ClientDisconnectError` branch of `isTerminalAbort` (the unique coverage) and letting #52365 handle the cron-timeout case via its proactive hook.

commands.author: simonusa

target.title: feat(gateway): wire coding tools into /tools/invoke HTTP surface
target.body:
## Summary

- `resolveGatewayScopedTools` only included `createOpenClawTools` (gateway management tools); `createOpenClawCodingTools` (exec, edit, read, browser, etc.) was never wired in
- The [official docs](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api#tools-invoke-api) describe `exec` as blocked by the HTTP deny list by default — implying the intent was always for coding tools to be available on this surface, gated by `gateway.tools.allow`
- This PR completes that wiring: coding tools are now reachable via `POST /tools/invoke`, subject to the existing deny list and policy pipeline
- Dedup: gateway tools take precedence over coding tools of the same name

## Root Cause

The deny list in `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` already included `exec`, `spawn`, `apply_patch`, etc. — designed to gate exactly these tools on the HTTP surface. But `createOpenClawCodingTools` was never passed to `resolveGatewayScopedTools`, so the deny list had nothing to gate: coding tools simply weren't present at all. The docs described the intended behavior; the implementation was incomplete.

## What did NOT change

- Security posture: `exec` and other high-risk tools remain on `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` — blocked by default, require explicit `gateway.tools.allow` to enable
- Agent session runners: no change to how tools are wired for LLM agent runs
- Tool policy pipeline: coding tools pass through the same `applyToolPolicyPipeline` + deny-list as all other tools

## Regression Test Plan

- Added `createOpenClawCodingTools` stub to the `vi.mock("../agents/pi-tools.js")` block in `tools-invoke-http.test.ts` — without this, all 22 existing tests failed with 500s (undefined function crash)
- Added new test: `exposes coding tools via /tools/invoke when agent policy allows them` — verifies a coding tool passes through policy + deny-list filtering and is callable via the HTTP surface

```
Tests  24 passed (24)  src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Before this PR, `POST /tools/invoke` did not expose any of the coding-primitive tools (`read`, `write`, `edit`, `exec`, `process`, `apply_patch`) — operators wanting deterministic one-shot tool calls had to go through a full agent turn. The PR wires `read` into the HTTP surface via a new unwrapped factory `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw()` (commit 1), then adds `write`, `edit`, `process` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` and excludes provider-gated `apply_patch` from factory output (commit 2). Operators opt in to mutating tools via `gateway.tools.allow`.
- **Real environment tested:** Docker image built fresh from this PR's branch head — `feat/tools-invoke-coding-tools-upstream` at `8673dcca85`, rebased onto current `upstream/main`. Build performed inside a running container based on `openclaw:main` with the PR's source tree mounted at `/workspace`; ran `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` (4m 45s, 794 packages) then `pnpm build:docker` (tsdown + postbuild, ~6 min). Final 92 MB `dist/` produced. Verified the deployed bundle contains both `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw` (in `pi-tools-*.js` and `tool-resolution-*.js`) and the expanded `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` including `process`, `write`, `edit` (in `dangerous-tools-*.js`). Gateway started via `node /workspace/openclaw.mjs gateway run` on port 19101 with a minimal token-auth config; no plugins, no models needed for the HTTP surface.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
# (1) Inside a node:22-bookworm-slim-compatible container with PR source mounted at /workspace
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build:docker

# (2) Start the gateway from the fresh build
node /workspace/openclaw.mjs gateway run

# (3) Run six curl probes against /tools/invoke for each tool name
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19101/tools/invoke \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"tool":"<TOOLNAME>","args":{...},"sessionKey":"main"}'

# (4) For the opt-in test, set gateway.tools.allow=["write"] in openclaw.json, restart, retry write
```

- **Evidence after fix (live curl outputs from the rebuilt gateway, all redacted token):**

```text
# 1. READ  (commit 1 — newly wired into HTTP surface by createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw)
$ curl POST /tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"read","args":{"path":"/tmp/proof-target.txt"},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"hello from pr-63919 proof at 2026-05-17T21:29:43Z
"}]}}
HTTP 200

# 2. WRITE  (commit 2 — added to DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY)
$ curl POST /tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"write","args":{"path":"/tmp/x","content":"x"},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
HTTP 404

# 3. EDIT  (commit 2)
$ curl POST /tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"edit","args":{...},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
HTTP 404

# 4. PROCESS  (commit 2)
$ curl POST /tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"process","args":{...},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: process"}}
HTTP 404

# 5. APPLY_PATCH  (commit 2 — excluded from factory as provider-gated)
$ curl POST /tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"apply_patch","args":{},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: apply_patch"}}
HTTP 404

# 6. WRITE opt-in  (commit 2 — gateway.tools.allow=["write"] removes from default deny)
# After editing openclaw.json: "gateway":{"tools":{"allow":["write"]}} and restarting
$ curl POST /tools/invoke -d '{"tool":"write","args":{"path":"/tmp/opt-in-success.txt","content":"opt-in works"},"sessionKey":"main"}'
{"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 12 bytes to /tmp/opt-in-success.txt"}]}}
HTTP 200

# Verify the file actually got written
$ cat /tmp/opt-in-success.txt
opt-in works
```

The deployed `dist/dangerous-tools-*.js` deny list also confirms the new entries from commit 2 are present:

```text
const DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY = [
  "exec",
  "spawn",
  "shell",
  "process",       // ← added by commit 2
  "write",         // ← added by commit 2
  "edit",          // ← added by commit 2
  "fs_write",
  "fs_delete",
  "fs_move",
  "apply_patch",
  "sessions_spawn",
  "sessions_send",
  "cron",
  "gateway",
  "nodes"
];
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Every assertion in the PR validated against the rebased branch:
  - `read` reachable via `/tools/invoke` with no agent turn, returning the actual file contents (commit 1 working — `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw()` wires read into the HTTP surface).
  - `write`, `edit`, `process`, `apply_patch` all default-denied with HTTP 404 (commit 2 working — three names added to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`, `apply_patch` excluded from the unwrapped factory because provider-gated).
  - `gateway.tools.allow=["write"]` correctly removes `write` from the effective deny set; the next curl returns 200 and the file actually appears on disk inside the container (commit 2's opt-in path working).
- **What was not tested:** Plugin-driven tools (`browser`, `web-search`) — out of scope; they already worked over `/tools/invoke` via the existing `createOpenClawTools` factory and are unchanged. The `nodes` plugin-suppression branch (also part of `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw`'s intent) is exercised by unit tests in `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` but not in this minimal-config gateway because no `nodes` plugin is configured. Cross-PR interaction with the merged RPC `tools.invoke` (#74804) — both surfaces now share `tool-resolution.ts` logic and the rebased PR resolves cleanly against it; covered by `tools-invoke-http.test.ts` `does not double-fire runBeforeToolCallHook` test.
- **Before evidence:** Same six curls against an unpatched OpenClaw `2026.4.x` (or `simon-prod-current` which lacks commit 2) return `200 + Successfully wrote N bytes` for `write` (because the deny-list didn't yet contain it) — i.e. the **opposite** of the fixed behavior. Verified independently against the pre-PR `openclaw:main` image earlier in the validation cycle. The `read` curl against an unpatched gateway returns `404 not_found` because `read` was never wired into `createOpenClawTools` for the HTTP path before this PR.

## Human Verification

- `npx vitest run src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` — 24/24 pass on a clean branch based on `upstream/main`
- Pre-commit hooks (tsgo, oxlint, lint:auth) passed

## Relationship to existing PRs

- #55906 (`fix(gateway): deny nodes over HTTP tools invoke`) — orthogonal: restricts the surface; this PR expands it. Both coexist.
- #55724 (`fix(gateway): enforce owner-only tools in invoke API`) — orthogonal: enforces owner-only policy. Our change passes `senderIsOwner: true` for HTTP bearer auth, consistent with that direction.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** operator enables `exec` via `gateway.tools.allow` without understanding the RCE surface  
  **Mitigation:** `exec` is on `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` — explicit opt-in required; no behavior change for default installs
- **Risk:** dedup logic (gateway tools shadow coding tools of the same name) could suppress a tool unexpectedly  
  **Mitigation:** gateway and coding tool names are disjoint in practice; merge order is documented in the code comment

Addresses #37131

target.title: fix(config): share subagent model schema
target.body:
Fixes #83291

Summary:
- Reuse the shared agent model schema for per-agent and tools subagent model config, including timeoutMs.
- Wire tools.subagents.model into spawned-subagent model selection and fallback precedence without changing cron isolated-run fallback semantics.
- Add regression coverage, generated config metadata, docs, and changelog.

Verification:
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-fallback-policy.test.ts src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
- pnpm config:docs:check
- pnpm docs:check-mdx
- git diff --check
- .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local

Behavior addressed: per-agent and tools subagent model config now accepts the shared model object shape, including timeoutMs, and spawned subagents use the configured model and fallbacks.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Node 22 toolchain.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-fallback-policy.test.ts src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts

What was not tested: live spawned-subagent provider calls against external model providers.
